title: The Sacred Sounds
subtitle: Meet the Devanagari vowels — the building blocks of Sanskrit
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize and pronounce all 13 Sanskrit vowels in Devanagari script, and understand why Sanskrit is called a 'perfectly phonetic' language.

concepts.heading: Why Devanagari?
concepts.explanation: Devanagari literally means 'script of the divine city.' Unlike English, every letter represents exactly one sound — no silent letters, no ambiguity. What you see is what you say. The Gita was composed to be heard, and Devanagari preserves those sounds perfectly.

concepts.heading: Short Vowels (ह्रस्व)
concepts.explanation: These are held for one beat. Think of them as quick, crisp sounds.

examples.meaning: like 'u' in 'but' — the most common sound in Sanskrit
a vowel 'l' — extremely rare, mostly theoretical

concepts.heading: Long Vowels (दीर्घ)
concepts.explanation: These are held for two beats — simply stretch the short vowel longer. In IAST, a macron (line above) marks them: ā, ī, ū.

examples.meaning: like 'a' in 'father' — as in आत्मा (ātmā, the Self)
like 'oo' in 'food' — as in रूप (rūpa, form)

concepts.heading: Diphthongs (संध्यक्षर)
concepts.explanation: Two vowel sounds blended into one. These are always long.

examples.meaning: like 'ay' in 'say' — as in एव (eva, indeed)
like 'ai' in 'aisle' — as in ऐश्वर्य (aiśvarya, sovereignty)
like 'ow' in 'how' — as in औषधि (auṣadhi, medicine)

concepts.heading: The Sacred Syllable OM (ॐ)
concepts.explanation: OM contains three sounds: अ (a) + उ (u) + म (m). It begins many Gita chapters and represents the ultimate reality (Brahman). You just learned the vowels that make it up!

examples.meaning: The primordial sound — BG 7.8: 'I am the syllable OM'

gita_connection: In BG 7.8, Krishna says: 'praṇavaḥ sarva-vedeṣu' — 'I am the syllable OM in all the Vedas.' The vowel अ (a) is considered the origin of all sounds. Krishna says in BG 10.33: 'akṣarāṇām akāro'smi' — 'Among letters, I am the letter A.' You just learned the most sacred sounds in the tradition.

practice.instruction: Look at each Devanagari vowel and say its IAST equivalent aloud. Cover the answers and test yourself.
Which vowel do you see in these Gita words? Identify the initial vowel.

recap: You learned all 13 Sanskrit vowels — 5 short, 4 long, and 4 diphthongs. Sanskrit is perfectly phonetic: one letter = one sound, always. The sacred syllable OM combines three of these vowels.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll learn the 25 consonant stops — and discover why Sanskrit organizes sounds more systematically than any other language on Earth.

title: The Consonant Grid
subtitle: 25 stops organized by mouth position — Sanskrit's scientific genius
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to read the 25 stop consonants (sparśa) organized in the 5×5 grid, and understand how mouth position and aspiration create the system.

concepts.heading: The 5×5 Grid — Sound Organized Scientifically
concepts.explanation: Sanskrit organizes 25 consonants into a 5×5 grid. Rows go by mouth position (throat → lips). Columns go by voicing and aspiration. No other ancient language did this. Each row is called a varga (class).

concepts.heading: Guttural (कवर्ग) — Throat sounds
concepts.explanation: Made at the back of the throat, like English 'k' and 'g'. The 5 columns are: unvoiced, unvoiced aspirated, voiced, voiced aspirated, nasal.

examples.meaning: aspirated k — like 'k' in 'khaki' with a puff of air
aspirated g — as in घोर (ghora, terrible)
like 'ng' in 'sing' — never starts a word

concepts.heading: Palatal (चवर्ग) — Roof-of-mouth sounds
concepts.explanation: Made with the tongue touching the hard palate, like English 'ch' and 'j'.

examples.meaning: like 'j' in 'joy' — as in जन्म (janma, birth)
like 'ny' in 'canyon' — as in ज्ञान (jñāna, knowledge)

concepts.heading: Retroflex (टवर्ग) — Curled-tongue sounds
concepts.explanation: Made by curling the tongue back to touch the roof of the mouth. Unique to Indian languages. In IAST, marked with a dot below: ṭ, ḍ, ṇ.

concepts.heading: Dental (तवर्ग) — Teeth sounds
concepts.explanation: Made with the tongue touching the upper teeth. The 'default' t/d in Sanskrit — softer than English.

examples.meaning: dental t — tongue on teeth, not the ridge
aspirated dental d — as in धर्म (dharma)

concepts.heading: Labial (पवर्ग) — Lip sounds
concepts.explanation: Made with the lips, like English 'p' and 'b'.

examples.meaning: like 'm' in 'man' — as in मन (mana, mind)

gita_connection: In BG 10.33, Krishna says: 'akṣarāṇām akāro'smi' — 'Among letters, I am A.' The consonant grid reflects the Gita's teaching of divine order (ṛta) in the universe — even sounds follow a perfect system. When you see भगवद्गीता (Bhagavad Gītā), you now know: भ is labial voiced aspirated, ग is guttural voiced.

practice.instruction: Identify the varga (class) of each consonant — is it guttural, palatal, retroflex, dental, or labial?
For each pair, identify which is aspirated and which is not.
Write the IAST for these Gita consonants.

recap: You learned the 25 stop consonants organized in a brilliant 5×5 grid: 5 mouth positions × 5 voicing/aspiration patterns. Aspirated consonants have a puff of air. Retroflexes curl the tongue back. This grid is Sanskrit's greatest phonetic achievement.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll complete the consonant inventory with semivowels, sibilants, and two special marks — anusvara and visarga — that appear constantly in the Gita.

title: Completing the Sound System
subtitle: Semivowels, sibilants, and the special marks that appear everywhere in the Gita
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize all remaining consonants (semivowels, sibilants, aspirate) plus visarga and anusvara — completing your knowledge of the full Devanagari alphabet.

concepts.heading: Semivowels (अन्तःस्थ) — Between Vowels and Consonants
concepts.explanation: These four sounds are 'in-between' — they flow like vowels but start like consonants. Each one corresponds to a vowel: इ→य, ऋ→र, ऌ→ल, उ→व.

examples.meaning: a soft, tapped 'r' — as in राम (Rāma)
like 'l' in 'love' — as in लोक (loka, world)

concepts.heading: Sibilants (ऊष्मन्) — The Hissing Sounds
concepts.explanation: Three 's' sounds, each made at a different mouth position. English has only one 's' — Sanskrit has three! In IAST, ś has an acute accent, ṣ has a dot below.

examples.meaning: palatal 's' — like 'sh' in 'ship' — as in शान्ति (śānti, peace)
retroflex 's' — tongue curled back — as in कृष्ण (Kṛṣṇa)
dental 's' — like English 's' in 'sun' — as in संसार (saṃsāra)

concepts.heading: The Aspirate
concepts.explanation: A pure breath sound, like a sigh.

examples.meaning: like 'h' in 'home' — as in हरि (Hari, a name of Viṣṇu)

concepts.heading: Anusvara (अनुस्वार) — The Nasal Dot
concepts.explanation: A dot above a letter (ं) that nasalizes the sound. In IAST it's written as ṃ. It appears constantly in the Gita — you'll see it in almost every verse. It takes the nasal quality of whatever consonant follows.

examples.meaning: I — as in BG 10.20: 'aham ātmā' (I am the Self)

concepts.heading: Visarga (विसर्ग) — The Breath Mark
concepts.explanation: Two dots after a letter (ः) — a soft 'h' sound with an echo of the preceding vowel. In IAST it's written as ḥ. Extremely common at the end of Sanskrit words and in sandhi.

examples.meaning: salutation — as in 'namaḥ te' (salutations to you)
suffering, sorrow — a key Gita concept

gita_connection: The visarga and anusvara appear in nearly every line of the Gita. For instance, BG 2.11 begins: 'aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṃ prajñāvādāṃś ca bhāṣase' — notice the anusvara in tvaṃ and the visarga implied throughout. Recognizing these marks is essential for reading any Gita verse.

practice.instruction: Identify whether each character is a semivowel, sibilant, or special mark.
Write the IAST for these characters.
Find the anusvara (ṃ) and visarga (ḥ) in these Gita words.

recap: You now know the complete Devanagari alphabet: 13 vowels, 25 stops, 4 semivowels, 3 sibilants, 1 aspirate, plus anusvara (ṃ) and visarga (ḥ). That's every sound in Sanskrit. The alphabet is done!
next_preview: Tomorrow is where the magic happens — you'll learn how vowels attach to consonants using mātrā marks. This is the key to reading actual Devanagari text.

title: Vowel Marks — Unlocking Real Reading
subtitle: How vowels attach to consonants with mātrā — and reading your first real words
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to read simple Devanagari syllables and real Sanskrit words like कृष्ण (Kṛṣṇa), अर्जुन (Arjuna), and गीता (Gītā).

concepts.heading: The Inherent 'a' — Sanskrit's Default Vowel
concepts.explanation: Every consonant in Devanagari has a built-in 'a' sound. When you see क, it's not just 'k' — it's 'ka'. This is the most important rule in Devanagari. You only write a separate vowel sign when the vowel is NOT 'a'.

examples.meaning: NOT just 'k' — the 'a' is built in

concepts.heading: Vowel Marks (मात्रा) — Changing the Built-in Vowel
concepts.explanation: To change the default 'a' to another vowel, you add a mātrā (vowel mark) to the consonant. Each vowel has its own mark shape. Here they are attached to क (ka) as an example.

examples.meaning: long ā — a vertical stroke after the consonant
short i — a hook BEFORE the consonant (written left)
long ī — a hook after the consonant
short u — a hook below the consonant
long ū — a tail below the consonant
vowel ṛ — a small hook below
au — two strokes above AND one after

concepts.heading: Reading Real Gita Words
concepts.explanation: Now let's put it together. Break each word into consonant + vowel mark syllables.

examples.meaning: क (ka) + ृ (ṛ) = कृ; ष्ण = ṣṇa → Kṛṣṇa (the dark one)
अ (a); र् (r) + ज (ja) = र्जु; न (na) → Arjuna (the bright one)
क (ka) + ु (u) = कु; र (ra) + ु (u) = रु → Kuru (the dynasty)
प (pa) + ा (ā) = पा; ण्ड (ṇḍa); व (va) → sons of Pāṇḍu

gita_connection: The very title भगवद्गीता (Bhagavad Gītā) uses mātrā marks everywhere. भ (bha), ग (ga), व (va), द् (d with no vowel), गी (gī with long ī mātrā), ता (tā with long ā mātrā). You can now decode the title letter by letter! It means 'The Song of the Lord.'

practice.instruction: Read these syllables aloud and write their IAST.
Sound out these Gita words syllable by syllable and write the full IAST.
What vowel mark (mātrā) is used in the highlighted syllable?

recap: You learned the mātrā system — how vowels attach to consonants as marks rather than standalone letters. The inherent 'a' is built into every consonant. You can now read real Devanagari words from the Gita like गीता, कृष्ण, and अर्जुन.
next_preview: Tomorrow tackles conjunct consonants — what happens when two consonants meet without a vowel between them. This is the key to reading words like क्षेत्र (kṣetra) and ज्ञान (jñāna).

title: Conjunct Consonants
subtitle: When consonants combine — cracking the code of Sanskrit's complex clusters
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize common conjunct consonants in the Gita, understand how the virāma works, and decode cluster combinations like क्ष, ज्ञ, and त्र.

concepts.heading: The Virāma (हलन्त) — Killing the Inherent Vowel
concepts.explanation: Remember, every consonant has a built-in 'a'. The virāma (a small diagonal stroke below: ्) removes it. So क = ka, but क् = just k. When two consonants lose the vowel between them, they merge into a conjunct.

examples.meaning: k with no vowel — the virāma (्) kills the inherent 'a'
bare 't' — often seen at the end of words like भगवत् (bhagavat)

concepts.heading: How Conjuncts Form
concepts.explanation: When consonant + virāma + consonant occur, they typically fuse into one combined shape. Some are intuitive (the first letter loses its vertical stroke), others are unique ligatures you just have to learn. The good news: about 10 conjuncts cover most of what you see in the Gita.

examples.meaning: t + r — as in मन्त्र (mantra, sacred formula)
d + v — as in द्वन्द्व (dvandva, pair of opposites)
n + d — as in आनन्द (ānanda, bliss)

concepts.heading: Essential Gita Conjuncts
concepts.explanation: These special ligatures appear constantly in the Gita. Memorize these — they are unique shapes that don't look like their component letters.

examples.meaning: k + ṣ — as in क्षेत्र (kṣetra, field) in BG 13.1
j + ñ — as in ज्ञान (jñāna, knowledge) — one of the Gita's most important words
ś + r — as in श्रद्धा (śraddhā, faith)
d + dh — as in बुद्धि (buddhi, intellect)
t + t — as in तत्त्व (tattva, truth/principle)
t + y — as in सत्य (satya, truth)

concepts.heading: The Special Behavior of 'r' (र)
concepts.explanation: The letter र (ra) behaves uniquely in conjuncts. When र comes BEFORE another consonant, it appears as a hook above (called repha): र्क = rka. When र comes AFTER another consonant, it appears as a small diagonal below: प्र = pra, क्र = kra.

examples.meaning: r before m — the hook (repha) sits above म
r after p — the small stroke below प
r after b, then h + m conjunct — 'the Absolute'
r before m — repha over म — 'righteousness'

gita_connection: BG 13.1 begins: 'idaṃ śarīraṃ kaunteya kṣetram ity abhidhīyate' — 'This body, O son of Kuntī, is called the field (kṣetra).' The word क्षेत्र contains the conjunct क्ष that you just learned. And ज्ञान (jñāna, knowledge) appears over 60 times in the Gita — it's one of the text's central concepts.

practice.instruction: Break these conjuncts into their component consonants.
Read these Gita words aloud, paying attention to the conjuncts.
Is the र (ra) a repha (above) or a subscript (below) in these words?

recap: Conjunct consonants form when two consonants meet without a vowel between them. The virāma removes the inherent 'a'. Some conjuncts like क्ष and ज्ञ are unique shapes you must memorize. The letter र has special repha (above) and subscript (below) forms.
next_preview: Tomorrow is reading day! You'll put everything together to read complete words from the Gita — धर्म, कर्म, योग, आत्मा, ब्रह्म — and learn how IAST transliteration maps to Devanagari.

title: Reading Real Sanskrit
subtitle: Putting it all together — reading actual Gita words with confidence
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to read 20+ key Sanskrit words from the Gita in Devanagari, transliterate them to IAST, and understand the systematic correspondence between script and sound.

concepts.heading: The IAST System — Your Transliteration Key
concepts.explanation: The International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration (IAST) is the standard Roman-letter system for Sanskrit. Every diacritical mark tells you something specific: macron (ā, ī, ū) = long vowel, dot below (ṭ, ḍ, ṇ, ṣ) = retroflex, accent (ś) = palatal sibilant, tilde-like (ñ) = palatal nasal.

concepts.heading: Core Gita Vocabulary — Philosophical Terms
concepts.explanation: These words are the backbone of the Gita's message. Read each one in Devanagari, check against the IAST, and note the meaning.

concepts.heading: Core Gita Vocabulary — Characters and Concepts
concepts.explanation: The people and ideas you'll encounter again and again in the Gita.

examples.meaning: the divine teacher, avatar of Viṣṇu

concepts.heading: Reading Strategy
concepts.explanation: When you encounter a Devanagari word: (1) identify each consonant, (2) check for mātrā marks that change the vowel, (3) watch for conjuncts and the virāma, (4) look for anusvara/visarga, (5) sound it out left to right. Sanskrit is perfectly phonetic — if you can identify the parts, you can read it.

examples.meaning: भ-ग-व-द्-गी-ता: The Song of the Lord — try sounding it out!
कु-रु-क्षे-त्र: The field of the Kurus — the battlefield
म-हा-भा-र-त: The great epic containing the Gita

gita_connection: Every word you just read comes directly from the Gita. धर्म appears in the very first verse: 'dharmakṣetre kurukṣetre' (BG 1.1) — 'On the field of dharma, on the field of the Kurus.' You can now read the two most important words in the Gita's opening line in their original script.

practice.instruction: Read each Devanagari word and write its IAST transliteration without looking at the answers.
Match the IAST to the correct Devanagari from memory.
Read these compound words from the Gita. Break them into syllables first, then transliterate.

recap: You can now read real Sanskrit words from the Gita in Devanagari. You know 20+ key terms and can transliterate them to IAST. The reading strategy — identify consonants, check mātrā, watch for conjuncts, sound it out — works for any Sanskrit word.
next_preview: Tomorrow is the Week 1 grand finale — you'll read your first complete Gita verse: BG 2.47, the famous 'karmaṇy evādhikāras te.' Every. Single. Syllable.

title: Your First Complete Verse
subtitle: Reading BG 2.47 — the most quoted verse in the Gita — syllable by syllable
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to sound out every syllable of Bhagavad Gita 2.47 in Devanagari, the most famous verse about selfless action.

concepts.heading: The Verse: BG 2.47
concepts.explanation: This is arguably the most quoted verse of the Gita. Krishna tells Arjuna the secret of action: you have a right to act, but never to the fruits of action. Let's read it in the original Sanskrit.

examples.iast: karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana
examples.meaning: Your right is to action alone, never to its fruits at any time
Let not the fruit of action be your motive, nor let your attachment be to inaction

concepts.heading: Line 1 Breakdown: कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते
concepts.explanation: Let's decode this word by word. In Sanskrit, words often merge together (sandhi) — we'll learn about that next week. For now, let's just identify the syllables.

concepts.heading: Line 1 continued: मा फलेषु कदाचन
concepts.explanation: The second half of line 1.

concepts.heading: Line 2 Breakdown: मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूः
concepts.explanation: The first half of line 2.

examples.meaning: fruit of action — a compound word: karma + phala

concepts.heading: Line 2 continued: मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि
concepts.explanation: The final quarter of the verse.

gita_connection: BG 2.47 is the heart of Karma Yoga — the yoga of selfless action. Krishna teaches Arjuna (and us) that we should act without attachment to results. This verse has inspired leaders from Mahatma Gandhi to modern mindfulness practitioners. You just read it in the original Sanskrit!

practice.instruction: Read the first line of BG 2.47 aloud, syllable by syllable. Point to each syllable as you read. Don't worry about speed — accuracy matters.
items: मा फलेषु कदाचन → mā pha-le-ṣu ka-dā-ca-na

practice.instruction: Now read the second line of BG 2.47 aloud.
items: मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि → mā te saṅ-go-stv-a-kar-ma-ṇi

practice.instruction: Match each word to its meaning from memory.

recap: Congratulations — you just read the most famous verse of the Bhagavad Gita in its original Sanskrit! You identified every syllable of BG 2.47 using the Devanagari skills from this week: vowels, consonants, mātrā marks, conjuncts, anusvara, and visarga. Week 1 is complete.
next_preview: Week 2 shifts from script to grammar. Day 8 tackles sandhi — the system of sound merging that makes Sanskrit words flow together. It's why 'karmaṇi eva' becomes 'karmaṇyeva' — and once you understand it, Sanskrit stops looking intimidating.

title: Sandhi — The Art of Sound Merging
subtitle: Why Sanskrit words flow together — and how to pull them apart
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize and reverse the most common sandhi rules in the Gita, understanding how separate words merge at their boundaries.

concepts.heading: What Is Sandhi?
concepts.explanation: Sandhi (संधि) literally means 'joining.' When Sanskrit words are placed next to each other, the sounds at word boundaries change according to precise rules. This is why Sanskrit can look like one long unbroken chain of letters. But once you know the rules, you can split the chain back into individual words. Think of it as the opposite of spelling — instead of memorizing exceptions, you learn predictable patterns.

examples.meaning: i + e → ye — the short i becomes y before a vowel
a + a → ā — two short a's merge into one long ā

concepts.heading: Vowel Sandhi (अच्-संधि) — The Big Three Rules
concepts.explanation: When two vowels meet at a word boundary, they combine. These three rules cover most cases in the Gita.

examples.meaning: Similar simple vowels merge: 'na + api → nāpi' (not even)
a + i/ī merge into e: 'na + iti → neti' (not so)
a + u/ū merge into o: 'sa + uktam → soktam' (thus said)

concepts.heading: Vowel Sandhi — Semivowel Changes
concepts.explanation: When i, u, or ṛ appear before a different vowel, they convert to their semivowel form.

examples.meaning: i/ī before a different vowel → y: 'iti + uvāca → ityuvāca'
u/ū before a different vowel → v: 'madhu + ari → madhvari'

concepts.heading: Visarga Sandhi
concepts.explanation: The visarga (ḥ) at the end of a word changes based on what follows. This is extremely common — almost every verse in the Gita has visarga sandhi.

examples.meaning: ḥ before c/ch → ś: visarga becomes palatal sibilant
ḥ before t/th → s: visarga becomes dental sibilant
aḥ before a vowel → o (with avagraha ऽ showing the dropped a)

concepts.heading: The Avagraha (ऽ) — The Missing Letter Mark
concepts.explanation: When an initial 'a' is dropped in sandhi, the avagraha sign (ऽ) marks where it was. It's like an apostrophe showing something was removed.

gita_connection: Look again at BG 2.47: 'karmaṇyevādhikāraste.' This is actually four separate words merged by sandhi: karmaṇi + eva + adhikāraḥ + te. The i+e → ye rule and aḥ+te → aste rule are both at work. Sandhi is why the Gita looks like continuous text — but now you can reverse-engineer it.

practice.instruction: Split these sandhi forms back into their original separate words.
Identify the type of sandhi in each example: vowel sandhi or visarga sandhi?
items: नैतत् — vowel sandhi (na + etat: a + e → ai)
तस्मादपि — vowel sandhi (tasmāt + api: t + a, but note consonant change too)

recap: Sandhi is Sanskrit's system of sound-merging at word boundaries. The three main vowel sandhi rules (a+a→ā, a+i→e, a+u→o) plus semivowel changes and visarga sandhi cover most of what you'll encounter in the Gita. The avagraha (ऽ) marks a dropped initial 'a'.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll learn the 8 cases of Sanskrit nouns — the reason Sanskrit doesn't need fixed word order. This is the key to understanding WHO does WHAT to WHOM in any Gita verse.

title: Nouns and the Eight Cases
subtitle: How Sanskrit marks WHO does WHAT to WHOM — without needing word order
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize the 8 Sanskrit cases (vibhakti) and identify the most common case endings in Gita verses, understanding how word order is freed by case markers.

concepts.heading: Why Cases Matter
concepts.explanation: In English, word order tells you who did what: 'Arjuna sees Krishna' vs 'Krishna sees Arjuna.' In Sanskrit, word endings do this job instead. The ending on a noun tells you its role in the sentence. This means words can appear in any order for poetic or emphatic effect — and the Gita uses this freedom constantly.

examples.meaning: Arjuna sees Krishna — ḥ marks the doer, ṃ marks the object
Same meaning! Word order changed, but case endings preserve the meaning

concepts.heading: The 8 Cases (विभक्ति)
concepts.explanation: Sanskrit has 8 cases. You don't need to master all of them — focus on the first three, which cover most of what you'll see in the Gita.

examples.meaning: रामः गच्छति — Rāma goes (Rāma is the doer)
रामं पश्यति — sees Rāma (Rāma is the object)

concepts.heading: Common Masculine Endings (-a stems)
concepts.explanation: Most masculine nouns in the Gita end in -a. Here are the singular case endings — these alone will unlock a huge portion of the text.

concepts.heading: Gita Cases in Action
concepts.explanation: Let's see how cases work in actual Gita verses.

examples.meaning: BG 1.1: 'In the field of dharma, in the field of the Kurus' — both locative (-e = in)

gita_connection: The very first words of the Gita are 'dharmakṣetre kurukṣetre' (BG 1.1) — both words in the locative case (-e ending = 'in'). The king asks: 'In the field of dharma, in the field of the Kurus, what did they do?' The -e ending instantly tells you WHERE the action takes place.

practice.instruction: Identify the case of each word based on its ending.
Translate these simple phrases using case knowledge.
items: कृष्णम् अर्जुनः पश्यति → Arjuna sees Krishna (kṛṣṇam = object)
योगेन मुक्तिः → Liberation by yoga (yogena = by means of)
धर्मस्य रक्षणम् → Protection of dharma (dharmasya = of)

recap: Sanskrit uses 8 cases to mark the role of every noun — subject, object, instrument, and more. This frees word order for poetic expression. The key endings for masculine -a stems are: -aḥ (doer), -am (object), -ena (by), -asya (of), -e (in). These five endings unlock a huge portion of the Gita.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll learn Sanskrit verbs — the action words. With just five key verb roots and their present tense forms, you'll be able to identify what's happening in most Gita verses.

title: Verbs — The Action Words
subtitle: Present tense basics and the key verbs of the Gita
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize present tense verb endings for third-person forms, and identify 10 key verb roots that appear throughout the Gita.

concepts.heading: Sanskrit Verb Basics
concepts.explanation: Sanskrit verbs encode person (who), number (how many), and tense/mood (when/how). The verb root (dhātu) is like a seed — endings grow from it. The good news: for reading the Gita, you mainly need to recognize third-person forms ('he/she/it does' and 'they do') since most verses describe actions of Krishna, Arjuna, or the Self.

examples.meaning: to do, to make — the most important Gita verb (karma comes from this root!)

concepts.heading: Present Tense Endings (लट्-लकार)
concepts.explanation: The present tense endings for the most common verb class. Focus on third person — that's what you'll see most in the Gita.

examples.meaning: 3rd person singular: 'he/she/it does' — गच्छति (gacchati, he goes)
3rd person plural: 'they do' — गच्छन्ति (gacchanti, they go)
2nd person singular: 'you do' — गच्छसि (gacchasi, you go)
1st person singular: 'I do' — गच्छामि (gacchāmi, I go)

concepts.heading: 10 Essential Gita Verb Roots
concepts.explanation: These verb roots appear again and again in the Gita. Knowing them unlocks the action in most verses.

examples.meaning: to do, to make — 'karma' comes from this root
to know — 'Veda' comes from this root
to think — 'manas' (mind) comes from this root
to yoke, to unite — 'yoga' comes from this root!

concepts.heading: Recognizing Verbs in the Gita
concepts.explanation: In Gita verses, the verb is often the last word. Look for the -ti, -nti, or -te endings.

examples.meaning: BG 2.20: 'It is not born, nor does it die' — both -te endings = verbs
BG 11.5: 'See my yoga!' — paśya is an imperative (command form)
'The Blessed Lord said' — uvāca is past tense of √vac (speak)

gita_connection: In BG 2.20, Krishna describes the eternal Self: 'na jāyate mriyate vā kadācit' — 'It is not born, nor does it ever die.' Both jāyate (is born, from √jan) and mriyate (dies, from √mṛ) end in -te, marking them as verbs. Spotting verb endings is your fastest path to understanding a verse's action.

practice.instruction: Identify the verb in each phrase by looking for -ti, -nti, -te, or -si endings.
Match each verb form to its root and meaning.

recap: Sanskrit verbs encode person and number through endings. The key endings are: -ti (he/she), -nti (they), -te (middle voice he/she), -si (you), -mi (I). Ten essential verb roots — √kṛ, √gam, √vid, √bhū, √vad, √dṛś, √man, √tyaj, √yuj, √jan — cover most of the action in the Gita.
next_preview: Tomorrow is vocabulary power day — you'll learn the 50 most frequent words in the Gita. With the script, sandhi, cases, and verbs you already know, adding vocabulary will let you understand significant portions of actual verses.

title: The Gita's Essential Vocabulary
subtitle: 50 words that unlock the meaning of the Bhagavad Gita
objective: By the end of today, you will know the 50 most important and frequently occurring words in the Gita, organized by category, giving you the vocabulary to follow the text's core arguments.

concepts.heading: The Self, God, and Reality
concepts.explanation: These are the Gita's 'big picture' words — the concepts that define its philosophical vision.

examples.meaning: the Self, the soul — the Gita's most central concept
the Lord, God, the supreme controller
the Blessed Lord — how Krishna is addressed
illusion, the power that conceals reality

concepts.heading: The Three Paths
concepts.explanation: The Gita teaches three main paths to liberation. These words define them.

examples.meaning: action, deed — and the path of selfless action
knowledge, wisdom — the path of understanding
devotion — the path of loving surrender

concepts.heading: Mind, Intellect, and Qualities
concepts.explanation: The Gita's psychological vocabulary — how the inner world works.

examples.meaning: mind — the faculty of thinking and feeling
quality — the three: sattva, rajas, tamas

concepts.heading: Ethics and Cosmic Order
concepts.explanation: Words about right action, duty, and the moral framework of the Gita.

examples.meaning: fruit, result — as in 'fruit of action'

concepts.heading: States and Goals
concepts.explanation: Where the spiritual journey leads.

examples.meaning: liberation, freedom from the cycle of rebirth
peace — the Gita ends with 'oṃ tat sat śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ'
the cycle of birth and death

concepts.heading: Common Structural Words
concepts.explanation: Small but mighty — these connectors and particles appear on nearly every line.

examples.meaning: thus, so — marks the end of a quote or thought
that — as in 'tat tvam asi' (that you are)

gita_connection: With these 50 words, you can follow the major arguments of the Gita. For example, BG 2.22: 'vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya' — knowing that vāsāṃsi means 'garments,' jīrṇāni means 'worn out,' and deha means 'body,' you can grasp Krishna's famous metaphor: the soul changes bodies like a person changes clothes.

practice.instruction: Cover the meanings and test yourself. How many can you recall?
Group these words by category: Path, Quality, or Goal.
Translate these common Gita phrases using your new vocabulary.

recap: You now know the 50 most essential Gita words across 6 categories: the divine (ātman, brahman, īśvara), the three paths (karma, jñāna, bhakti), the inner world (manas, buddhi, guṇa), ethics (dharma, satya, śraddhā), goals (mokṣa, śānti), and structural particles (ca, na, eva, hi). These words are the Gita's vocabulary backbone.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll learn Sanskrit's compound word system — how small words combine into massive compounds. This is the key to decoding long Sanskrit words that look intimidating but are actually just building blocks stacked together.

title: Compound Words — Sanskrit's Building Blocks
subtitle: How to decode long Sanskrit words by breaking them into parts
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to recognize the 4 main types of Sanskrit compound words (samāsa) and break down long Gita compounds into their meaningful components.

concepts.heading: Why Compounds Matter
concepts.explanation: Sanskrit loves to join words together into compounds — sometimes 3, 4, or even 10 words fused into one. This is why Sanskrit can look intimidating. But every compound follows predictable rules. Once you learn the 4 types, you can reverse-engineer any compound into its parts.

examples.meaning: karma + phala = 'fruit of action' — two simple words joined
mahā + ātmā = 'great soul' — Mahatma Gandhi's title!

concepts.heading: Type 1: Tatpuruṣa (Determinative) — 'X of Y'
concepts.explanation: The most common type. The first word modifies the second, with an implied case relationship (of, for, by, in, from). The last word is the main word. Think of it as 'right-headed' — the meaning lives on the right.

examples.meaning: karma + yoga = 'yoga of action' — a central Gita teaching

concepts.heading: Type 2: Dvandva (Copulative) — 'X and Y'
concepts.explanation: Two or more equal items joined together, meaning 'A and B.' Both words have equal weight.

concepts.heading: Type 3: Karmadhāraya (Descriptive) — 'X that is Y'
concepts.explanation: An adjective-noun compound where the first word describes the second. Like Tatpuruṣa but with an 'is' relationship instead of 'of.'

examples.meaning: mahā + ātmā = 'great soul' (the soul that IS great)
sat + guru = 'true teacher' (teacher who IS true)
parama + ātmā = 'supreme Self' (the Self that IS supreme)

concepts.heading: Type 4: Bahuvrīhi (Possessive) — 'One who has X'
concepts.explanation: The trickiest type. The compound describes someone who POSSESSES the quality, not the quality itself. The meaning points OUTSIDE the compound to a person or thing.

examples.meaning: mahā + bāhu = 'one who has mighty arms' — an epithet for Arjuna
catur + bhuja = 'one who has four arms' — an epithet for Viṣṇu
padma + nābha = 'one whose navel is a lotus' — an epithet for Viṣṇu

concepts.heading: Breaking Down Compounds — Your Strategy
concepts.explanation: When you see a long word: (1) Look for words you already know inside it. (2) Try splitting at natural points. (3) Ask: is this 'X of Y' (tatpuruṣa), 'X and Y' (dvandva), 'X that is Y' (karmadhāraya), or 'one who has X' (bahuvrīhi)?

examples.meaning: bhagavat + gītā = 'song of the Lord' — tatpuruṣa!
kuru + kṣetra = 'field of the Kurus' — tatpuruṣa!

gita_connection: The Gita's title itself is a tatpuruṣa compound: भगवद्गीता (bhagavad + gītā) = 'song of the Blessed Lord.' BG 2.48 uses the compound 'yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi' — 'established in yoga, perform actions.' The compound yogasthaḥ = yoga + stha = 'standing in yoga' — a bahuvrīhi meaning 'one who stands in yoga.'

practice.instruction: Break these compounds into their parts and identify the type.
items: कर्मयोग → karma + yoga = yoga of action (tatpuruṣa)
सुखदुःख → sukha + duḥkha = pleasure and pain (dvandva)
आत्मज्ञान → ātma + jñāna = knowledge of Self (tatpuruṣa)

practice.instruction: Build the compound from these parts and give the meaning.

recap: Sanskrit compounds join words into larger units. The four types are: Tatpuruṣa ('X of Y' — most common), Dvandva ('X and Y'), Karmadhāraya ('X that is Y'), and Bahuvrīhi ('one who has X'). Breaking compounds apart is your key to decoding long Sanskrit words.
next_preview: Tomorrow you'll do a complete word-by-word analysis of BG 2.20, one of the Gita's most beautiful verses about the eternal soul. You'll use every skill you've learned: script, sandhi, cases, verbs, vocabulary, and compounds.

title: Full Verse Analysis
subtitle: Dissecting BG 2.20 word by word — the eternal soul verse
objective: By the end of today, you will be able to perform a complete analysis of a Gita verse — reading the Devanagari, splitting sandhi, identifying cases and verb forms, and arriving at the meaning through your own understanding of the Sanskrit.

concepts.heading: The Verse: BG 2.20
concepts.explanation: This verse describes the ātman (Self) as eternal and unchanging. Krishna uses six verbs to say what the Self does NOT do. It's one of the most philosophically important verses in the Gita.

examples.devanagari: न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः
examples.iast: na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ
examples.meaning: It is not born, nor does it ever die; having come to be, it will not come to be again

examples.devanagari: अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे
examples.iast: ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre
examples.meaning: Unborn, eternal, everlasting, primeval — it is not slain when the body is slain

concepts.heading: Line 1a: न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचित्
concepts.explanation: Word-by-word analysis of the first quarter.

examples.meaning: is born — from √jan (to be born), present middle voice, 3rd sg.
dies — from √mṛ (to die), present middle voice, 3rd sg.
ever, at any time — kada (when) + cit (ever)

concepts.heading: Line 1b: नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः
concepts.explanation: Sandhi note: नायं = न + अयम् (na + ayam, 'not this one'). The a+a→ā vowel sandhi rule!

examples.meaning: not + this one — vowel sandhi: a + a → ā

concepts.heading: Line 2a: अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणः
concepts.explanation: A string of adjectives describing the ātman. Sandhi note: शाश्वतोऽयं = शाश्वतः + अयम् (aḥ + a → o + avagraha).

examples.meaning: unborn — a (not) + ja (born). Nominative case: describes the subject

concepts.heading: Line 2b: न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे
concepts.explanation: The grand conclusion — even when the body is slain, the Self is not slain.

examples.meaning: is not slain — from √han (to kill), passive voice: 'is killed'
when being slain — present passive participle, locative case: 'when X is being killed'
body — locative case (-e ending): 'in/when the body'

concepts.heading: Grammar Summary
concepts.explanation: Skills you used to read this verse: Devanagari reading (Week 1), sandhi splitting (na+ayam→nāyam, śāśvataḥ+ayam→śāśvato'yam), case identification (nominative -aḥ for adjectives, locative -e for 'when/in'), verb recognition (-te endings), and vocabulary (na, vā, ca). Everything connects.

gita_connection: BG 2.20 is one of the Gita's philosophical cornerstones. Krishna is teaching Arjuna that the true Self (ātman) cannot be destroyed. The verse uses 6 forms of 'not' — na jāyate (not born), na mriyate (not dying), na bhavitā (will not become again), ajaḥ (unborn), na hanyate (not slain). This relentless negation is a powerful rhetorical technique: defining the infinite by what it is NOT.

practice.instruction: Read the full verse aloud in Devanagari, syllable by syllable. Then read it again more fluidly.
items: नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः

practice.instruction: Without looking at the analysis above, identify the sandhi in these forms and split them.
items: शाश्वतोऽयम् → शाश्वतः + अयम् (aḥ + a → o with avagraha)

practice.instruction: Identify the grammatical form of each word.
items: जायते → verb, present middle, 3rd singular (is born)
शरीरे → noun, locative singular (in/when the body)
हन्यमाने → participle, passive, locative (when being slain)

recap: You just performed a complete analysis of BG 2.20 — one of the most important verses in the Gita. You read Devanagari, split sandhi, identified case endings and verb forms, and understood the meaning directly from the Sanskrit. Every skill from the past 13 days came together.
next_preview: Tomorrow is the final day — your graduation challenge. You'll read three of the Gita's most famous verses independently: the garment metaphor (2.22), the divine descent (4.7), and the ultimate surrender (18.66). You're ready.

title: The Final Challenge
subtitle: Read three famous Gita verses on your own — you've earned this
objective: By the end of today, you will independently read and appreciate three of the most beloved verses of the Bhagavad Gita in their original Sanskrit, using every skill you've built over the past two weeks.

concepts.heading: Verse 1: BG 2.22 — The Garment Metaphor
concepts.explanation: Krishna compares the soul changing bodies to a person changing clothes. This is one of the Gita's most accessible and beautiful metaphors.

examples.devanagari: वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि
examples.iast: vāsāṃsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya navāni gṛhṇāti naro'parāṇi
examples.meaning: Just as a person discards worn-out garments and puts on new ones

examples.devanagari: तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णान्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही
examples.iast: tathā śarīrāṇi vihāya jīrṇāny anyāni saṃyāti navāni dehī
examples.meaning: So the embodied soul discards worn-out bodies and enters new ones

concepts.heading: BG 2.22 — Word by Word
concepts.explanation: Let's break down the key words. Try to identify them yourself before reading the explanations.

examples.meaning: worn out, old — neuter plural adjective agreeing with vāsāṃsi
just as ... so — a comparison pair (learn this pattern!)
takes, puts on — from √grah (to seize/take), 3rd sg. present
the embodied one (the soul that dwells in a body) — nominative singular

concepts.heading: Verse 2: BG 4.7 — The Divine Descent
concepts.explanation: Krishna reveals why he incarnates in every age — whenever dharma declines and adharma rises. This verse is the foundation of the avatāra doctrine.

examples.devanagari: यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत
examples.iast: yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata
examples.meaning: Whenever there is a decline of dharma, O Bhārata
And a rise of adharma — then I create myself (I incarnate)

concepts.heading: BG 4.7 — Word by Word
concepts.explanation: Notice how the grammar reveals the meaning precisely.

examples.meaning: whenever, whenever — the repetition adds emphasis: WHENEVER
comes to be, happens — from √bhū, 3rd sg. present
rise, ascendancy — a compound: abhi + ut + √sthā (standing up against)
I create, I manifest — from √sṛj, 1st person singular (-mi ending!)

concepts.heading: Verse 3: BG 18.66 — The Ultimate Surrender
concepts.explanation: The Gita's grand finale. Krishna's final teaching: let go of everything and take refuge in the divine alone. This is called the carama śloka (final/ultimate verse) by many traditions.

examples.iast: sarvadharmān parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja
examples.meaning: Abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in me alone

examples.devanagari: अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः
examples.iast: ahaṃ tvā sarvapāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
examples.meaning: I shall liberate you from all sins — do not grieve

concepts.heading: BG 18.66 — Word by Word
concepts.explanation: Every grammar concept you've learned is here: compounds, cases, verbs, sandhi.

examples.meaning: all dharmas — sarva+dharma compound, accusative plural (object)
having abandoned — absolutive of pari+√tyaj (completely letting go)
alone, only — accusative, agreeing with mām
go! take! — imperative of √vraj (to go), 2nd person sg.
from all sins — sarva+pāpa compound, ablative plural (-ebhyaḥ = 'from')
I shall liberate — future tense, 1st person, causative of √muc (to free)
do not grieve — mā (don't) + śucaḥ (grieve, from √śuc)

concepts.heading: What You've Achieved
concepts.explanation: In 14 days, you went from zero Sanskrit to reading three of the Gita's most famous verses in the original. You can now: read Devanagari script, apply IAST transliteration, split sandhi, recognize case endings, identify verb forms, parse compounds, and appreciate the beauty of the original Sanskrit. You haven't just learned about Sanskrit — you've started reading it.

gita_connection: These three verses span the Gita's entire arc: BG 2.22 (Chapter 2) teaches the eternal nature of the soul, BG 4.7 (Chapter 4) reveals why the divine incarnates, and BG 18.66 (Chapter 18) delivers the ultimate teaching of surrender. Together, they capture the Gita's essence: the Self is eternal, God descends to restore dharma, and liberation comes through surrender. You can now appreciate all three in the language they were composed in.

practice.instruction: Read all three verses aloud in Devanagari. Take your time. Let the sounds flow.
items: BG 2.22: वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि। तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णान्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही॥
BG 4.7: यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत। अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्॥
BG 18.66: सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज। अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः॥

practice.instruction: For each verse, identify: (a) the main verb, (b) one sandhi example, (c) one compound word.
items: BG 2.22: verb = गृह्णाति (gṛhṇāti, takes); sandhi = नरोऽपराणि (naraḥ + aparāṇi); compound = शरीराणि (not a compound, but जीर्णानि is an adjective)
BG 4.7: verb = भवति (bhavati, happens) and सृजामि (sṛjāmi, I create); sandhi = ग्लानिर्भवति (glāniḥ + bhavati); compound = अभ्युत्थानम् (abhi+ut+sthāna, rising up)
BG 18.66: verb = व्रज (vraja, go!) and मोक्षयिष्यामि (mokṣayiṣyāmi, I shall liberate); sandhi = मामेकम् (mām + ekam); compound = सर्वधर्मान् (sarva+dharma, all dharmas)

practice.instruction: Final practice: Close your eyes, take a breath, and recite any one of these three verses from memory — or as close to memory as you can manage. Even a partial recitation is a triumph.
items: Choose your favorite of the three verses and recite it.

recap: You've completed the 14-day Sanskrit journey. You read three of the Gita's most iconic verses — the garment metaphor (2.22), the divine descent (4.7), and the ultimate surrender (18.66) — using skills you've built from scratch: Devanagari script, IAST transliteration, vowel marks, conjuncts, sandhi, cases, verbs, vocabulary, and compounds. You can now approach any Gita verse and begin to unlock its meaning in the original Sanskrit.
next_preview: Your Sanskrit foundation is built. From here, you can deepen your journey: study more grammar, learn additional vocabulary, or simply continue reading the Gita verse by verse — each one will be a little easier than the last. The original Sanskrit is no longer a closed door. You have the key.
